---
title: "Validation"
format: 
  revealjs:
    incremental: true
    callout-icon: false
    code-overflow: scroll
    code-block-height: 200px
    #theme: [default, ../custom.scss]
#julia:
#  path: "/home/emmanuel/.juliaup/bin/julia"
smaller: false
css: ../../../styles.css
#filters:
  #- parse-latex
---



# Goodness of Fit (R²)

::: {style="display:none"}
$\newcommand{\VS}{\quad \mathrm{VS} \quad}$
$\newcommand{\and}{\quad \mathrm{and} \quad}$
$\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb E}$
$\newcommand{\P}{\mathbb P}$
$\newcommand{\Var}{\mathbb V}$
$\newcommand{\1}{\mathbf 1}$
:::


## Pythagorean Decomposition

. . .

Let $\mathbf{1}$ be the constant column vector in $\mathbb R^{n\times 1}$.

. . .

If [$\mathbf{1} \in [X]$]{style="background-color: yellow;"} (eg if we consider an [intercept]{style="background-color: yellow;"})
$$ \underbrace{\|Y-\overline Y \1\|^2}_{SST} = \underbrace{\|Y-\widehat Y\|^2}_{SSR}+\underbrace{\|\widehat Y-\overline Y \1\|^2}_{SSE}$$

In the general case,

. . .

$$\|Y\|^2 = \|Y-\widehat Y\|^2 + \|\widehat Y\|^2$$

[Good model]{style="background-color: yellow;"} if sum of squares of residuals [$SSR \ll SST$ ]{style="background-color: yellow;"}


## R²

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
::: {.callout-note}
## R² if 1 ∈ [X]
$$R^2 = \frac{SSE}{SST} = 1-\frac{SSR}{SST}$$
:::
:::
::: {.column}
::: {.callout-note}
## R² if 1 ∉ [X]

$$R^2 = \frac{\|\widehat Y\|^2}{\|Y\|^2} = 1 - \frac{SSR}{\|Y\|^2}$$
:::
::: 

:::

- $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$. Better model if $R^2$ close to $1$
- Two definitions of $R^2$ when $\1 \in [X]$ or not
- In simple linear regression $(Y_i = \beta_1+\beta_2X_i+\varepsilon_i)$: $R^2 = \hat \rho^2$ is the [square empirical correlation]{style="background-color: yellow;"} between $Y$ and $X$

## Adjusted R²

. . .

Main flaw of $R^2$: [adding a new variable increases $R^2$]{style="background-color: yellow;"} (because $[X]$ is a bigger projection space)

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
::: {.callout-note}
## Rₐ² if 1 ∈ [X]
$$R^2_a = 1-\frac{n-1}{n-p}\frac{SSR}{SST}$$
:::
:::
::: {.column}
::: {.callout-note}
## Rₐ² if 1 ∉ [X]

$$R^2_a = 1 - \frac{n}{n-p}\frac{SSR}{\|Y\|^2}$$
:::
::: 

:::

. . .

With a [new variable]{style="background-color: yellow;"}, $SSR$ decreases but [$p \to p+1$]{style="background-color: yellow;"}

. . .

[$R_a^2$​ only increases]{style="background-color: yellow;"} when adding a new variable [if that variable significantly]{style="background-color: yellow;"} reduces the residual sum of squares.


## R² always rises, R²ₐ does not {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size:86%"}
Start from one real predictor, then add **pure-noise** columns. $R^2$ (blue) **keeps rising** toward 1 — that's its flaw; the **adjusted** $R^2_a$ (green) peaks and then falls, penalising useless variables.
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.lm-btn{font-family:inherit;font-size:.85em;padding:.4em 1.1em;margin:.12em .22em;border:1px solid #bbb;border-radius:8px;background:#f4f4f4;color:#333;cursor:pointer}
.lm-row{font-size:.72em;line-height:2.2;margin:.12rem 0}
.lm-wrap{text-align:center}
.lm-sw{display:inline-block;width:11px;height:11px;border-radius:2px;vertical-align:middle}
</style>
<div class="lm-wrap">
  <canvas id="raCanvas" width="600" height="300" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;max-width:100%"></canvas>
  <div class="lm-row">junk predictors added k <input id="raK" type="range" autocomplete="off" min="0" max="24" step="1" value="6" style="vertical-align:middle;width:180px"> <b id="raKv">6</b> &nbsp;·&nbsp; noise <input id="raNz" type="range" autocomplete="off" min="0.2" max="2" step="0.05" value="0.8" style="vertical-align:middle;width:90px"> <b id="raNzv">0.80</b> &nbsp; <button class="lm-btn" id="raNew">↻ resample</button></div>
  <div class="lm-row"><span class="lm-sw" style="background:#5b8cff"></span> R² <b id="raR2">–</b> &nbsp; <span class="lm-sw" style="background:#27ae60"></span> R²ₐ <b id="raR2a">–</b> &nbsp;·&nbsp; p = 2+k = <b id="raP">–</b></div>
</div>
<script>
(function () {
  var cv = document.getElementById('raCanvas'); if (!cv || cv.dataset.init) return; cv.dataset.init = 1;
  var c = cv.getContext('2d'), W = cv.width, H = cv.height, N = 30, KM = 24, Xr = [], J = [], Z = [], r2 = [], r2a = [];
  var kEl = document.getElementById('raK'), nzEl = document.getElementById('raNz');
  function gauss() { var u = 0, v = 0; while (!u) u = Math.random(); while (!v) v = Math.random(); return Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * v); }
  function solve(Mx, b) { var n = b.length, a = Mx.map(function (r) { return r.slice(); }), bb = b.slice(), i, r, k; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { var pv = i; for (r = i + 1; r < n; r++) if (Math.abs(a[r][i]) > Math.abs(a[pv][i])) pv = r; var t = a[i]; a[i] = a[pv]; a[pv] = t; var tb = bb[i]; bb[i] = bb[pv]; bb[pv] = tb; for (r = i + 1; r < n; r++) { var f = a[r][i] / (a[i][i] || 1e-12); for (k = i; k < n; k++) a[r][k] -= f * a[i][k]; bb[r] -= f * bb[i]; } } var x = new Array(n).fill(0); for (i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) { var s = bb[i]; for (k = i + 1; k < n; k++) s -= a[i][k] * x[k]; x[i] = s / (a[i][i] || 1e-12); } return x; }
  function regen() { Xr = []; Z = []; for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) { Xr.push(-1 + 2 * i / (N - 1)); Z.push(gauss()); } J = []; for (var k = 0; k < KM; k++) { var col = []; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) col.push(gauss()); J.push(col); } }
  function compute() {
    var nz = +nzEl.value, Y = [], yb = 0, i, k; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { Y.push(1.5 * Xr[i] + nz * Z[i]); yb += Y[i]; } yb /= N;
    var sst = 0; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) sst += (Y[i] - yb) * (Y[i] - yb);
    r2 = []; r2a = [];
    for (k = 0; k <= KM; k++) {
      var p = 2 + k, cols = []; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { var row = [1, Xr[i]]; for (var j = 0; j < k; j++) row.push(J[j][i]); cols.push(row); }
      var M = [], v = []; for (var r = 0; r < p; r++) { M.push(new Array(p).fill(0)); v.push(0); }
      for (i = 0; i < N; i++) for (r = 0; r < p; r++) { for (var cc = 0; cc < p; cc++) M[r][cc] += cols[i][r] * cols[i][cc]; v[r] += cols[i][r] * Y[i]; }
      for (r = 0; r < p; r++) M[r][r] += 1e-8; var beta = solve(M, v), ssr = 0;
      for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { var yh = 0; for (r = 0; r < p; r++) yh += beta[r] * cols[i][r]; ssr += (Y[i] - yh) * (Y[i] - yh); }
      r2.push(1 - ssr / sst); r2a.push(1 - (N - 1) / (N - p) * ssr / sst);
    }
  }
  function draw() {
    var k = +kEl.value; document.getElementById('raKv').textContent = k; document.getElementById('raNzv').textContent = (+nzEl.value).toFixed(2);
    var pad = 34, X0 = pad, Y0 = 12, BW = W - pad - 12, BH = H - pad - 18;
    function PX(kk) { return X0 + kk / KM * BW; } function PY(y) { return Y0 + (1 - y) * BH; }
    c.clearRect(0, 0, W, H);
    c.strokeStyle = '#eee'; c.lineWidth = 1; c.fillStyle = '#999'; c.font = '10px sans-serif'; c.textAlign = 'right';
    [0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1].forEach(function (g) { c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(X0, PY(g)); c.lineTo(X0 + BW, PY(g)); c.stroke(); c.fillText(g.toFixed(2), X0 - 4, PY(g) + 3); });
    c.textAlign = 'center'; c.fillText('k (junk predictors)', X0 + BW / 2, H - 4);
    function curve(arr, col) { c.strokeStyle = col; c.lineWidth = 2.5; c.beginPath(); for (var i = 0; i <= KM; i++) { var x = PX(i), y = PY(Math.max(0, arr[i])); i ? c.lineTo(x, y) : c.moveTo(x, y); } c.stroke(); }
    curve(r2, '#5b8cff'); curve(r2a, '#27ae60');
    c.strokeStyle = '#bbb'; c.setLineDash([4, 3]); c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(PX(k), Y0); c.lineTo(PX(k), Y0 + BH); c.stroke(); c.setLineDash([]);
    [[r2, '#5b8cff'], [r2a, '#27ae60']].forEach(function (q) { c.fillStyle = q[1]; c.beginPath(); c.arc(PX(k), PY(Math.max(0, q[0][k])), 4, 0, 6.2832); c.fill(); });
    document.getElementById('raR2').textContent = r2[k].toFixed(3); document.getElementById('raR2a').textContent = r2a[k].toFixed(3); document.getElementById('raP').textContent = 2 + k;
  }
  kEl.addEventListener('input', draw);
  nzEl.addEventListener('input', function () { compute(); draw(); });
  document.getElementById('raNew').addEventListener('click', function () { regen(); compute(); draw(); });
  regen(); compute(); draw();
})();
</script>
```

## R output interpretation

```
Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-8.065 -3.107  0.152  3.495  9.587 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -36.9435     3.3651  -10.98 7.62e-12 ***
Girth         5.0659     0.2474   20.48  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 4.252 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9353,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9331 
```

Here, $R^2=0.9353$ and $R^2_a=0.9331$.

. . .

$\approx 93\%$ of the variability is explained by the model.

# Testing Linear Constraints on β

## Linear Constraints

. . .

We want to test **q linear constraints** on the coefficient vector $\beta \in \mathbb R^p$.

. . .

This is formulated as:

::: {.square-def}
$$H_0: R\beta = 0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: R\beta \neq 0$$

:::


where **R** is a $(q × p)$ constraint matrix encoding the restrictions, with $q \leq p$.

## Common Test Types

::: {.square-def}
$H_0: R\beta = 0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: R\beta \neq 0$

:::


- **Student's t-test**: is variable $j$ significant? \
[$H_0: \beta_j = 0$ vs $H_1: \beta_j \neq 0$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}
- **Global F-test**: is any variable significant? Identity matrix excluding intercept \
[$H_0: \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_p = 0$ vs $H_1: \exists j \in \{2,\ldots,p\}$ s.t. $\beta_j \neq 0$ ]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}
- **Nested model test**: are q variables jointly significant? \
[$H_0: \beta_{p-q+1} = \cdots = \beta_p = 0$ vs $H_1$: the contrary]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}


## Key Applications

- **Individual significance**: Testing if a single predictor matters
- **Overall model significance**: Testing if the model explains anything beyond the intercept  
- **Variable subset significance**: Testing if a group of variables contributes to the model

## Fisher Test

::: {.callout-note}
## Theorem

- $SSR$: sum of squares of residuals in the unconstrained regression model
- $SSR_c$: sum of squares of residuals in the constrained regression model, i.e., in the sub-model satisfying $R\beta = 0$
  
::: {.fragment}
If $\text{rank}(X) = p$, $\text{rank}(R)=q$ and $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2 I_n)$, then under $H_0: R\beta = 0$:

$$F = \frac{n-p}{q} \cdot \frac{{SSR}_c - {SSR}}{{SSR}} \sim F(q, n-p)$$

where $F(q, n-p)$ denotes the Fisher distribution with $(q, n-p)$ degrees of freedom. [Elements of proof](../lectures/validation.qmd#F-Test)
:::
:::


## Rejection Region

. . .

Key argument: $SSR_c - SSR$ is equal to $\|(P_{V_0^\perp}-P_{[X]^\perp})Y\|^2$, where [$V_0=X(Ker(R))$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"} and $\dim(V_0)=p-q$ (so $[X]\ominus V_0$ has dimension $q$).

. . .

Therefore, the critical region at significance level $\alpha$ for testing $H_0: R\beta = 0$ against $H_1: R\beta \neq 0$ is:

::: {.square-def}
$$RC_\alpha = \{F > f_{q,n-p}(1-\alpha)\}$$
:::



where $f_{q,n-p}(1-\alpha)$ denotes the $(1-\alpha)$-quantile of an $F(q, n-p)$ distribution.



## Particular Case 1: Student Test

. . .

Fix some variable $j$ and consider

::: {.square-def}
$H_0: \beta_j=0$ VS $H_1:\beta_j\neq 0$
:::



. . .

Only one constraint: $q=1$, so that

:::{style="font-size: 80%;"}
::: {.square-def}
$$ F = (n-p) \frac{SSR_c-SSR}{SSR} \sim \mathcal F(1,n-p) \sim \mathcal T^2(n-p)$$
:::
:::



. . .

In fact, Here $F = \big(\tfrac{\hat \beta_j}{\hat \sigma_{\hat \beta_j}}\big)^2$ so [$F$ is the squared student test statistic]{style="background-color: yellow;"} presented before


## Particular Case 2: Global Fisher Test

. . .

::: {.square-def}
$H_0: \beta_2= \dots = \beta_p=0$ VS $H_1$: contrary
:::

$q = p-1$ in this case

. . .

::: {.square-def}
$$F = \frac{n-p}{p-1}\frac{SSE}{SSR} = \frac{n-p}{p-1}\frac{R^2}{1-R^2} \sim \mathcal F(p-1, n-p)$$
:::

## Particular Case 3: Nested Fisher Test


. . .

::: {.square-def}
$H_0: \beta_{p-q+1}= \dots = \beta_p=0$ VS $H_1$: contrary
:::


. . .

::: {.square-def}
$$F = \frac{n-p}{q}\frac{SSR_c - SSR}{SSR} \sim \mathcal F(q, n-p)$$
:::

. . .

Interpretation:

If [$F \geq f_{q,n-p}(1-\alpha)$]{style="background-color: yellow;"} ($1-\alpha$-quantile of Fisher dist.) then constraints are not satisfied. We [do not accept the submodel]{style="background-color: yellow;"} with respect to larger model.


## Example of R Output

```

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -36.9435     3.3651  -10.98 7.62e-12 ***
Girth         5.0659     0.2474   20.48  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 4.252 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9353,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9331 
F-statistic: 419.4 on 1 and 29 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
```

. . .

Here, Fisher global significance test statistic is $F=419.4$, $q=1$ and $n-p=29$ ($n=31$). [pvalue is negligible]{style="background-color: yellow;"}

. . .

Here, $q=1$ and Global Fisher test is a [Student Test]{style="background-color: yellow;"}.


# Verification of Model Hypotheses

## Model Assumptions

The linear regression model relies on the following key assumptions:

- **Model specification**: $Y = X\beta + \varepsilon$ (linear relationship)
- **Full rank design**: $\text{rank}(X) = p$ (no perfect multicollinearity)
- **Zero mean errors**: $\mathbb{E}(\varepsilon) = 0$
- **Homoscedastic errors**: $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2 I_n$ (constant variance and uncorrelated errors)

. . .

Now: diagnostic tools to [verify each assumption]{style="background-color: yellow;"} and remedial strategies when they fail.


## Linearity: diagnostic tools

The linearity assumption $\mathbb{E}(Y) = X\beta$ is the fundamental hypothesis of linear regression.

**Diagnostic Tools:**

- **Pre-modeling**: Scatter plots $(X^{(j)}, Y)$ and empirical correlations for each predictor
- **Post-modeling**: Residual analysis - non-linearity manifests as patterns in $\hat{\varepsilon}$
- **Advanced**: Partial residual plots (not covered here)

## Linearity: remedies

The linearity assumption $\mathbb{E}(Y) = X\beta$ is the fundamental hypothesis of linear regression.

**Remedial Strategies:**

- **Transformations**: Apply transformations to $Y$ and/or predictors $X^{(j)}$ to achieve linearity
- **Alternative models**: If transformations fail, consider nonlinear regression models

## Full rank: why it matters

The condition $\text{rank}(X) = p$ ensures no predictor $X^{(j)}$ is a linear combination of others.

. . .

**Why it matters:**

- **Identifiability**: Without full rank, $\beta$ is not uniquely defined
- **Estimation**: $(X^TX)$ becomes non-invertible, making $\hat{\beta} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^TY$ undefined
 - Infinitely many solutions satisfy $X^TX\hat{\beta} = X^TY$

## Full rank: in practice

The condition $\text{rank}(X) = p$ ensures no predictor $X^{(j)}$ is a linear combination of others.

**In practice:**

- Perfect collinearity is rare, so $\text{rank}(X) = p$ usually holds
- **Near-collinearity** is the real concern - when predictors are "almost" linearly dependent

## Near-collinearity: consequences

When a variable is highly correlated with others (correlation close to but not exactly $\pm 1$):

. . .

**Mathematical consequences:**

- $X^TX$ remains invertible, but its smallest eigenvalue approaches zero
- $(X^TX)^{-1}$ becomes numerically unstable

## Near-collinearity: instability

. . .

**Statistical implications:**

- **Instability**: Adding/removing a single observation can drastically change $(X^TX)^{-1}$
- **Unreliable estimates**: $\hat{\beta} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^TY$ becomes highly unstable
- **Inflated variance**: $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}) = \sigma^2(X^TX)^{-1}$ becomes very large

. . .

This is undesirable from a statistical point of view

## Detecting Collinearity: VIF

Compute the VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) for each $X^{(j)}$:

1. Regress $X^{(j)}$ on all other $X^{(k)}$ (where $k \neq j$)
2. Compute $R_j^2$ from this regression
3. Calculate: $\text{VIF}_j = \frac{1}{1 - R_j^2}$

. . .

**Properties:**

- $\text{VIF}_j \geq 1$ always
- High VIF indicates collinearity. Common threshold: [$\text{VIF}_j \geq 5$]{style="background-color: yellow;"}. In R: `vif()` from `car` package


## VIF explodes near collinearity {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size:86%"}
Regress $X^{(2)}$ on $X^{(1)}$ (left): its $R_2^2$ rises with the correlation, and $\mathrm{VIF}_2=\dfrac{1}{1-R_2^2}$ (right) blows up — inflating $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta_2)$ by the same factor.
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.lm-btn{font-family:inherit;font-size:.85em;padding:.4em 1.1em;margin:.12em .22em;border:1px solid #bbb;border-radius:8px;background:#f4f4f4;color:#333;cursor:pointer}
.lm-row{font-size:.72em;line-height:2.3;margin:.12rem 0}
.lm-wrap{text-align:center}
</style>
<div class="lm-wrap">
  <canvas id="vfCanvas" width="600" height="280" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;max-width:100%"></canvas>
  <div class="lm-row">correlation r (X₁,X₂) <input id="vfR" type="range" autocomplete="off" min="0" max="0.99" step="0.01" value="0.5" style="vertical-align:middle;width:170px"> <b id="vfRv">0.50</b> &nbsp; <button class="lm-btn" id="vfNew">↻ resample</button></div>
  <div class="lm-row">R₂² = <b id="vfR2">–</b> &nbsp;·&nbsp; VIF₂ = 1/(1−R₂²) = <b id="vfVIF">–</b> &nbsp;·&nbsp; <span id="vfMsg">–</span></div>
</div>
<script>
(function () {
  var cv = document.getElementById('vfCanvas'); if (!cv || cv.dataset.init) return; cv.dataset.init = 1;
  var c = cv.getContext('2d'), W = cv.width, H = cv.height, N = 40, z1 = [], z2 = [];
  var rEl = document.getElementById('vfR');
  function gauss() { var u = 0, v = 0; while (!u) u = Math.random(); while (!v) v = Math.random(); return Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * v); }
  function regen() { z1 = []; z2 = []; for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) { z1.push(gauss()); z2.push(gauss()); } }
  function draw() {
    var r = +rEl.value, s = Math.sqrt(1 - r * r), i; document.getElementById('vfRv').textContent = r.toFixed(2);
    var X1 = [], X2 = []; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { X1.push(z1[i]); X2.push(r * z1[i] + s * z2[i]); }
    var m1 = 0, m2 = 0; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { m1 += X1[i]; m2 += X2[i]; } m1 /= N; m2 /= N;
    var sxy = 0, sxx = 0, syy = 0; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { sxy += (X1[i] - m1) * (X2[i] - m2); sxx += (X1[i] - m1) * (X1[i] - m1); syy += (X2[i] - m2) * (X2[i] - m2); }
    var R2 = sxy * sxy / ((sxx * syy) || 1e-9), VIF = 1 / Math.max(1e-6, 1 - R2), b1 = sxy / (sxx || 1e-9), b0 = m2 - b1 * m1;
    c.clearRect(0, 0, W, H); var gap = 26, pw = (W - gap) / 2, ph = H - 30, top = 8;
    // left: scatter X1 vs X2 + regression line
    var lx0 = 28, lx1 = pw - 6, V = 3; function LX(x) { return lx0 + (x + V) / (2 * V) * (lx1 - lx0); } function LY(y) { return top + (V - y) / (2 * V) * ph; }
    c.strokeStyle = '#ddd'; c.strokeRect(lx0, top, lx1 - lx0, ph); c.fillStyle = '#777'; c.font = '11px sans-serif'; c.textAlign = 'center'; c.fillText('X₂ vs X₁  (R₂²)', (lx0 + lx1) / 2, H - 8);
    c.strokeStyle = '#e74c3c'; c.lineWidth = 2; c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(LX(-V), LY(b0 + b1 * (-V))); c.lineTo(LX(V), LY(b0 + b1 * V)); c.stroke();
    c.fillStyle = 'rgba(44,62,80,0.7)'; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { c.beginPath(); c.arc(LX(X1[i]), LY(X2[i]), 2.8, 0, 6.2832); c.fill(); }
    // right: VIF curve vs r
    var rx0 = pw + gap, rx1 = W - 12, VM = 25; function RX(rr) { return rx0 + rr * (rx1 - rx0); } function RY(v) { return top + (1 - Math.min(v, VM) / VM) * ph; }
    c.strokeStyle = '#ddd'; c.strokeRect(rx0, top, rx1 - rx0, ph); c.fillStyle = '#777'; c.fillText('VIF vs r', (rx0 + rx1) / 2, H - 8);
    c.fillStyle = '#999'; c.font = '9px sans-serif'; c.textAlign = 'right'; [1, 5, 10, 25].forEach(function (g) { c.fillText(g, rx0 - 2, RY(g) + 3); c.strokeStyle = g === 5 ? '#f0c040' : '#eee'; c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(rx0, RY(g)); c.lineTo(rx1, RY(g)); c.stroke(); });
    c.strokeStyle = '#7c5cff'; c.lineWidth = 2.5; c.beginPath(); for (i = 0; i <= 100; i++) { var rr = i / 100 * 0.99, vv = 1 / Math.max(1e-6, 1 - rr * rr); i ? c.lineTo(RX(rr), RY(vv)) : c.moveTo(RX(rr), RY(vv)); } c.stroke();
    c.fillStyle = '#7c5cff'; c.beginPath(); c.arc(RX(Math.sqrt(R2)), RY(VIF), 4.5, 0, 6.2832); c.fill();   // empirical correlation -> point sits on the 1/(1-x^2) curve
    document.getElementById('vfR2').textContent = R2.toFixed(3); document.getElementById('vfVIF').textContent = VIF.toFixed(1);
    document.getElementById('vfMsg').innerHTML = VIF >= 5 ? '<span style="color:#e74c3c">VIF ≥ 5 → collinearity concern</span>' : '<span style="color:#1b7a3a">acceptable</span>';
  }
  rEl.addEventListener('input', draw);
  document.getElementById('vfNew').addEventListener('click', function () { regen(); draw(); });
  regen(); draw();
})();
</script>
```

## Remedies for Multicollinearity

- **Variable removal**: [Drop variables]{style="background-color: yellow;"} with high VIF
 - Preferably remove those [least correlated with $Y$]{style="background-color: yellow;"}
- **Penalized regression**: Ridge, LASSO, or elastic net methods (not covered here)

. . .

**Important Distinction on Multicollinearity**

- **Parameter estimation**: Multicollinearity severely affects $\hat{\beta}$ reliability
- **Prediction**: Not problematic: $\hat{Y}$ remains well-defined and stable since [projection on $[X]$ is still unique]{style="background-color: yellow;"}


## Analysis of the Residuals

. . .

Recall that [$\hat \varepsilon = Y - \widehat Y = P_{[X]^{\perp}} \varepsilon$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}

. . .

### Residual Properties

- $\E(\hat{\varepsilon}) = 0$
- $\Var(\hat{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^2P_{[X]}^{\perp}$
- $\text{Cov}(\hat{\varepsilon}, \hat{Y}) = 0$
- If $\1 \in [X]$: $\bar{\hat{\varepsilon}} = 0$

## Diagnostic Tools

1. **Graphical Assessment:** Visual evaluation of model quality

2. **Homoscedasticity Test:**
Test: $\Var(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ for all $i$ (constant variance)
3. **Non-correlation Test:**  
Test: $\Var(\varepsilon)$ is diagonal (uncorrelated errors)

4. **Normality Test:** Examine normality of residuals



## Residual vs. Fitted Plot

. . .

The [scatter plot between $\hat{Y}$ and $\hat{\varepsilon}$]{style="background-color: yellow;"} is informative.

Since $\text{Cov}(\hat{\varepsilon}, \hat{Y}) = 0$, [no structure should appear]{style="background-color: yellow;"}.

If patterns emerge, this may indicate violations of:

- Linearity assumption
- Homoscedasticity assumption  
- Non-correlation assumption
- Or a combination of these...

## Residual vs fitted: read the pattern {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size:86%"}
Left: data $(x,Y)$ with the **linear** fit. Right: the **residuals vs fitted** plot. When the model is right it's a structureless cloud; a **curve** signals non-linearity, a **funnel** signals heteroscedasticity.
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.lm-btn{font-family:inherit;font-size:.85em;padding:.4em 1.1em;margin:.12em .22em;border:1px solid #bbb;border-radius:8px;background:#f4f4f4;color:#333;cursor:pointer}
.lm-btn.on{background:#3b6fb6;color:#fff;border-color:#3b6fb6}
.lm-row{font-size:.72em;line-height:2.3;margin:.12rem 0}
.lm-wrap{text-align:center}
</style>
<div class="lm-wrap" id="rfWrap">
  <canvas id="rfCanvas" width="620" height="290" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;max-width:100%"></canvas>
  <div class="lm-row">
    <button class="lm-btn on" data-mode="ok">well specified</button>
    <button class="lm-btn" data-mode="nonlin">non-linear</button>
    <button class="lm-btn" data-mode="hetero">heteroscedastic</button>
    &nbsp; noise <input id="rfNz" type="range" autocomplete="off" min="0.1" max="1" step="0.05" value="0.35" style="vertical-align:middle;width:90px"> <b id="rfNzv">0.35</b> &nbsp; <button class="lm-btn" id="rfNew">↻ resample</button>
  </div>
</div>
<script>
(function () {
  var cv = document.getElementById('rfCanvas'); if (!cv || cv.dataset.init) return; cv.dataset.init = 1;
  var c = cv.getContext('2d'), W = cv.width, H = cv.height, N = 60, mode = 'ok', Xr = [], Z = [];
  var nzEl = document.getElementById('rfNz');
  function gauss() { var u = 0, v = 0; while (!u) u = Math.random(); while (!v) v = Math.random(); return Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * v); }
  function regen() { Xr = []; Z = []; for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) { Xr.push(-1.3 + 2.6 * Math.random()); Z.push(gauss()); } }
  function yOf(i) { var x = Xr[i], nz = +nzEl.value; if (mode === 'nonlin') return 0.3 + 0.6 * x + 0.9 * x * x + nz * Z[i]; if (mode === 'hetero') return 0.3 + 1.0 * x + nz * (0.3 + 1.4 * (x + 1.3) / 2.6) * Z[i] * 3; return 0.3 + 1.0 * x + nz * Z[i]; }
  function draw() {
    document.getElementById('rfNzv').textContent = (+nzEl.value).toFixed(2);
    var Y = [], i, mx = 0, my = 0; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { Y.push(yOf(i)); mx += Xr[i]; my += Y[i]; } mx /= N; my /= N;
    var sxy = 0, sxx = 0; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { sxy += (Xr[i] - mx) * (Y[i] - my); sxx += (Xr[i] - mx) * (Xr[i] - mx); }
    var b1 = sxy / sxx, b0 = my - b1 * mx, yh = [], res = []; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { yh.push(b0 + b1 * Xr[i]); res.push(Y[i] - yh[i]); }
    var ymin = 1e9, ymax = -1e9, rmax = 1e-6, yhmin = 1e9, yhmax = -1e9; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { ymin = Math.min(ymin, Y[i]); ymax = Math.max(ymax, Y[i]); rmax = Math.max(rmax, Math.abs(res[i])); yhmin = Math.min(yhmin, yh[i]); yhmax = Math.max(yhmax, yh[i]); }
    c.clearRect(0, 0, W, H); var gap = 24, pw = (W - gap) / 2, ph = H - 34, top = 8;
    // left: data + line
    var lx0 = 30, lx1 = pw - 6, xlo = -1.5, xhi = 1.5; function LX(x) { return lx0 + (x - xlo) / (xhi - xlo) * (lx1 - lx0); } function LY(y) { return top + (ymax + 0.2 - y) / ((ymax - ymin) + 0.4) * ph; }
    c.strokeStyle = '#ddd'; c.strokeRect(lx0, top, lx1 - lx0, ph); c.fillStyle = '#777'; c.font = '11px sans-serif'; c.textAlign = 'center'; c.fillText('data (x, Y) + linear fit', (lx0 + lx1) / 2, H - 6);
    c.strokeStyle = '#e74c3c'; c.lineWidth = 2; c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(LX(xlo), LY(b0 + b1 * xlo)); c.lineTo(LX(xhi), LY(b0 + b1 * xhi)); c.stroke();
    c.fillStyle = 'rgba(44,62,80,0.7)'; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { c.beginPath(); c.arc(LX(Xr[i]), LY(Y[i]), 2.6, 0, 6.2832); c.fill(); }
    // right: residual vs fitted
    var rx0 = pw + gap, rx1 = W - 10; function RX(x) { return rx0 + (x - (yhmin - 0.2)) / ((yhmax - yhmin) + 0.4) * (rx1 - rx0); } function RY(r) { return top + (rmax * 1.15 - r) / (2.3 * rmax) * ph; }
    c.strokeStyle = '#ddd'; c.strokeRect(rx0, top, rx1 - rx0, ph); c.fillStyle = '#777'; c.fillText('residuals vs fitted', (rx0 + rx1) / 2, H - 6);
    c.strokeStyle = '#aaa'; c.setLineDash([4, 3]); c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(rx0, RY(0)); c.lineTo(rx1, RY(0)); c.stroke(); c.setLineDash([]);
    c.fillStyle = 'rgba(231,76,60,0.7)'; for (i = 0; i < N; i++) { c.beginPath(); c.arc(RX(yh[i]), RY(res[i]), 2.6, 0, 6.2832); c.fill(); }
  }
  function setMode(m) { mode = m; Array.prototype.forEach.call(document.querySelectorAll('#rfWrap .lm-btn[data-mode]'), function (b) { b.classList.toggle('on', b.dataset.mode === m); }); draw(); }
  Array.prototype.forEach.call(document.querySelectorAll('#rfWrap .lm-btn[data-mode]'), function (b) { b.addEventListener('click', function () { setMode(b.dataset.mode); }); });
  nzEl.addEventListener('input', draw);
  document.getElementById('rfNew').addEventListener('click', function () { regen(); draw(); });
  regen(); draw();
})();
</script>
```

## Homoscedasticity test (Breusch–Pagan)


. . .

We want to test whether $\Var(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2, ~~\forall i$

. . .

**Principle**: Assume $\varepsilon_i$ has variance [$\sigma_i^2 = \sigma^2 + z_i^T\gamma$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"} where:

- [$z_i$ is a $k$-vector]{style="background-color: orange;"} of variables that might explain [heteroscedasticity]{style="background-color: orange;"} ([known]{style="background-color: yellow;"})
- **Default in R**: $z_i = (X_i^{(1)}, \ldots, X_i^{(p)})$, so $k = p$
- $\gamma$ is an [unknown]{style="background-color: yellow;"} $k$-dimensional parameter

. . .


. . .

$H_0: \gamma = 0$ ([homosced.]{style="background-color: lightgreen;"}) VS $H_1: \gamma \neq 0$ ([heterosced.]{style="background-color: orange;"})

. . .

**R function**: `bptest` from `lmtest` library

## Consequences of Heteroscedasticity

. . .

**What happens**:

- OLS estimation of $\beta$ is no longer optimal but remains consistent
- Inference tools (tests, confidence intervals) become invalid because they rely on $\hat{\sigma}^2$ estimation


## Solutions for Heteroscedasticity


- **Transformation**: Transform $Y$ (e.g., log transformation) to stabilize variance
- **Modeling**: Model heteroscedasticity explicitly and account for it in estimation
- **GLS**: Use Generalized Least Squares (not detailed here)


## Non-correlation of errors

. . .

**Purpose**: Test if $\Var(\varepsilon)$ is diagonal (uncorrelated errors)

. . .

Correlation between $\varepsilon_i$ [often occurs with temporal data]{style="background-color: yellow;"} (index $i$ represents time)

. . .

**Auto-correlation Model of order $r$**:

::: {.square-def}
$$\varepsilon_i = \rho_1 \varepsilon_{i-1} + \dots + \rho_r \varepsilon_{i-r} + \eta_i$$
where $\eta_i \sim \text{iid } N(0, \sigma^2)$
:::



## Tests for Correlation

. . .

In the auto-correlation model [$\varepsilon_i = \rho_1 \varepsilon_{i-1} + \dots + \rho_r \varepsilon_{i-r} + \eta_i$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}: 

. . .

**Durbin-Watson Test** (for $r = 1$ only):

- [$H_0: \rho_1 = 0$ VS $H_1: \rho_1 \neq 0$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}
- **R function**: `dwtest` from `lmtest`

. . .

**Breusch-Godfrey Test** (for any $r$):

- [$H_0: \rho_1 = \cdots = \rho_r = 0$ VS $H_1:$ at least one $\rho_j \neq 0$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}
- User chooses order $r$ (default $r = 1$)
- **R function**: `bgtest` from `lmtest`


## Consequences of Auto-correlation

- OLS estimation of $\beta$ is no longer optimal but remains consistent
- Inference tools (tests, confidence intervals) become invalid

. . .

**Solutions:**

- **GLS modeling**: Model the dependence structure (complex, risky if wrong)
- **Model improvement**: [Exploit the dependence]{style="background-color: yellow;"} to enhance the model
- **Ex**: [Explain $Y_i$ using $Y_{i-1}$]{style="background-color: yellow;"} in addition to $(X_i^{(1)}, \dots, X_i^{(p)})$

## Normality of residuals

. . .

**Purpose**: Examine normality of residuals $\hat \varepsilon$

**Reminder on Normality Assumption**

- **Not essential** when $n$ is large
- All tests remain **asymptotically valid**
- Only **prediction intervals** truly require normality

. . .


**Perhaps the least important assumption, but we can still examine it since**

If $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2 I_n)$ then [$\hat{\varepsilon} \sim N(0, \sigma^2 P_{[X]}^{\perp})$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}


## Diagnostic Tools for Normality of ε̂

. . .

**Q-Q Plot (Henry's line)**:

- Plot theoretical vs. sample quantiles of $\hat{\varepsilon}$
- **R function**: `qqnorm`

. . .

**Shapiro-Wilk, $\chi^2$ or KS Tests**:

- Formal test of normality for $\hat{\varepsilon}$
- $H_0$: residuals are normally distributed
- $H_1$: residuals are not normally distributed
- **R function**: `shapiro.test`


## Q–Q plot of the residuals {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size:86%"}
Sorted standardized residuals vs theoretical normal quantiles. **On the line** ⇒ normal; an **S-shape** ⇒ skew, **steep tails** ⇒ heavy tails. Pick the error distribution.
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.lm-btn{font-family:inherit;font-size:.85em;padding:.4em 1.1em;margin:.12em .22em;border:1px solid #bbb;border-radius:8px;background:#f4f4f4;color:#333;cursor:pointer}
.lm-btn.on{background:#3b6fb6;color:#fff;border-color:#3b6fb6}
.lm-row{font-size:.72em;line-height:2.3;margin:.12rem 0}
.lm-wrap{text-align:center}
</style>
<div class="lm-wrap" id="qqWrap">
  <canvas id="qqCanvas" width="360" height="320" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;max-width:100%"></canvas>
  <div class="lm-row">
    <button class="lm-btn on" data-mode="normal">normal</button>
    <button class="lm-btn" data-mode="skew">right-skewed</button>
    <button class="lm-btn" data-mode="heavy">heavy-tailed</button>
    &nbsp; N <input id="qqN" type="range" autocomplete="off" min="15" max="200" step="1" value="60" style="vertical-align:middle;width:100px"> <b id="qqNv">60</b> &nbsp; <button class="lm-btn" id="qqNew">↻ resample</button>
  </div>
</div>
<script>
(function () {
  var cv = document.getElementById('qqCanvas'); if (!cv || cv.dataset.init) return; cv.dataset.init = 1;
  var c = cv.getContext('2d'), W = cv.width, H = cv.height, MAXN = 200, mode = 'normal', U = [];
  var nEl = document.getElementById('qqN');
  function gauss() { var u = 0, v = 0; while (!u) u = Math.random(); while (!v) v = Math.random(); return Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * v); }
  function qnorm(p) { var a = [-3.969683028665376e1, 2.209460984245205e2, -2.759285104469687e2, 1.38357751867269e2, -3.066479806614716e1, 2.506628277459239], b = [-5.447609879822406e1, 1.615858368580409e2, -1.556989798598866e2, 6.680131188771972e1, -1.328068155288572e1], cc = [-7.784894002430293e-3, -3.223964580411365e-1, -2.400758277161838, -2.549732539343734, 4.374664141464968, 2.938163982698783], d = [7.784695709041462e-3, 3.224671290700398e-1, 2.445134137142996, 3.754408661907416], pl = 0.02425, q, r; if (p < pl) { q = Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(p)); return (((((cc[0] * q + cc[1]) * q + cc[2]) * q + cc[3]) * q + cc[4]) * q + cc[5]) / ((((d[0] * q + d[1]) * q + d[2]) * q + d[3]) * q + 1); } if (p <= 1 - pl) { q = p - 0.5; r = q * q; return (((((a[0] * r + a[1]) * r + a[2]) * r + a[3]) * r + a[4]) * r + a[5]) * q / (((((b[0] * r + b[1]) * r + b[2]) * r + b[3]) * r + b[4]) * r + 1); } q = Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(1 - p)); return -(((((cc[0] * q + cc[1]) * q + cc[2]) * q + cc[3]) * q + cc[4]) * q + cc[5]) / ((((d[0] * q + d[1]) * q + d[2]) * q + d[3]) * q + 1); }
  function regen() { U = []; for (var i = 0; i < MAXN; i++) U.push([Math.random(), Math.random(), gauss(), gauss(), gauss(), gauss()]); }   // stored randoms per index (nested in N)
  function sample(i) { var u = U[i]; if (mode === 'skew') return -Math.log(u[0]); if (mode === 'heavy') return u[2] / Math.sqrt((u[3] * u[3] + u[4] * u[4] + u[5] * u[5]) / 3); return u[2]; }   // skew=exp, heavy=t(3)
  function draw() {
    var n = +nEl.value, i; document.getElementById('qqNv').textContent = n;
    var s = []; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) s.push(sample(i)); var m = 0; s.forEach(function (v) { m += v; }); m /= n; var sd = 0; s.forEach(function (v) { sd += (v - m) * (v - m); }); sd = Math.sqrt(sd / (n - 1)) || 1;
    s = s.map(function (v) { return (v - m) / sd; }).sort(function (a, b) { return a - b; });
    var R = 3.4, pad = 30; function PX(x) { return pad + (x + R) / (2 * R) * (W - pad - 10); } function PY(y) { return H - pad - (y + R) / (2 * R) * (H - pad - 10); }
    c.clearRect(0, 0, W, H); c.strokeStyle = '#ddd'; c.strokeRect(pad, 10, W - pad - 10, H - pad - 10);
    c.strokeStyle = '#aaa'; c.setLineDash([5, 4]); c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(PX(-R), PY(-R)); c.lineTo(PX(R), PY(R)); c.stroke(); c.setLineDash([]);
    c.fillStyle = 'rgba(44,62,80,0.75)'; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { var th = qnorm((i + 0.5) / n); c.beginPath(); c.arc(PX(Math.max(-R, Math.min(R, th))), PY(Math.max(-R, Math.min(R, s[i]))), 2.6, 0, 6.2832); c.fill(); }
    c.fillStyle = '#777'; c.font = '11px sans-serif'; c.textAlign = 'center'; c.fillText('theoretical N(0,1) quantiles', W / 2 + 8, H - 6);
    c.save(); c.translate(12, H / 2); c.rotate(-Math.PI / 2); c.fillText('sample quantiles', 0, 0); c.restore();
  }
  function setMode(m) { mode = m; Array.prototype.forEach.call(document.querySelectorAll('#qqWrap .lm-btn[data-mode]'), function (b) { b.classList.toggle('on', b.dataset.mode === m); }); draw(); }
  Array.prototype.forEach.call(document.querySelectorAll('#qqWrap .lm-btn[data-mode]'), function (b) { b.addEventListener('click', function () { setMode(b.dataset.mode); }); });
  nEl.addEventListener('input', draw);
  document.getElementById('qqNew').addEventListener('click', function () { regen(); draw(); });
  regen(); draw();
})();
</script>
```

# Outliers and Influential Points

## Outlier Analysis

. . .

An individual is atypical when:

1. **Poorly explained** by the model, and/or
2. **Heavily influences** coefficient estimation

. . .

**Identify** these individuals to:

- **Understand** the reason for this particularity
- **Potentially modify** the model accordingly  
- **Potentially exclude** the individual from the study


## Poorly explained: standardized residuals

. . .

Individual $i$ is poorly explained if its residual [$\hat{\varepsilon}_i$ is "abnormally" large]{style="background-color: yellow;"}.

. . .

**How to quantify "abnormally"?**

Let [$h_{ij}$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"} be elements of matrix $P_{[X]}$ (hat matrix).

For a Gaussian model: [$\hat{\varepsilon}_i \sim N(0, (1-h_{ii})\sigma^2)$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}


. . .

**Standardized Residuals**

::: {.square-def}
$$t_i = \frac{\hat{\varepsilon}_i}{\hat{\sigma}\sqrt{1-h_{ii}}}$$
:::


## Poorly explained: the test

. . .

::: {.square-def}
$$h_{ij} = (P_{[X]})_{ij} \and t_i = \frac{\hat{\varepsilon}_i}{\hat{\sigma}\sqrt{1-h_{ii}}}$$

:::



. . .

We expect $t_i \sim St(n-p)$ (not strictly true since $\hat{\varepsilon}_i \not\perp \hat{\sigma}^2$)

::: {.callout-note}
## Definition
Individual $i$ is considered poorly explained by the model if:
$$|t_i| > t_{n-p}(1-\alpha/2)$$
for predetermined $\alpha$, typically $\alpha = 0.05$, giving $t_{n-p}(1-\alpha/2) \approx 2$.
:::



## Leverage Points

. . .

A point is influential if it [contributes significantly to $\hat{\beta}$]{style="background-color: yellow;"} estimation.

. . .

**Leverage value**: $h_{ii}$ corresponds to the weight of $Y_i$ on its own estimation $\hat{Y}_i$

. . .

We know that: $\sum_{i=1}^n h_{ii} = \text{tr}(P_{[X]}) = p$

. . .

Therefore, on average: $h_{ii} \approx p/n$

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
## Definition
Individual $i$ is called a **leverage point** if $h_{ii} \gg p/n$

Typically: $h_{ii} > 2p/n$ or $h_{ii} > 3p/n$
:::


## Outlier Analysis: Cook's Distance

. . .

**Cook's Distance**

Quantifies the influence of individual $i$ on $\hat{Y}$:

::: {.square-def}
$$C_i = \frac{\|\hat{Y} - \hat{Y}_{(-i)}\|^2}{p\hat{\sigma}^2}$$
:::


where $\hat{Y}_{(-i)} = X\hat{\beta}_{(-i)}$ with $\hat{\beta}_{(-i)}$: estimation of $\beta$ without individual $i$


## Cook's Distance, Alternative Formula

. . .

::: {.square-def}
$$C_i = \frac{1}{p} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{1-h_{ii}} \cdot t_i^2,$$
:::


where [$t_i = \frac{\hat{\varepsilon}_i}{\hat{\sigma}\sqrt{1-h_{ii}}}$]{style="background-color: lightblue;"}.

This formula shows that Cook's distance $C_i$ combines:

1. **Aberrant effect** of individual (through $t_i$)
2. **Leverage effect** (through $h_{ii}$)

. . .

**R functions**: `cooks.distance` and last plot of `plot.lm`


## Outlier, leverage, influence {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size:84%"}
**Drag the gold point.** OLS line (solid) vs the fit *without* it (dashed). **Outlier** = large residual $|t_i|>2$ (aberrant in $Y$). **Leverage** = extreme in $x$, $h_{ii}>2p/n$. **Influential** = it moves the fit: Cook's $C_i=\frac1p\frac{h_{ii}}{1-h_{ii}}t_i^2>4/n$ — a mixture of the two.
:::

```{=html}
<style>
.lm-btn{font-family:inherit;font-size:.85em;padding:.4em 1.1em;margin:.12em .22em;border:1px solid #bbb;border-radius:8px;background:#f4f4f4;color:#333;cursor:pointer}
.lm-row{font-size:.72em;line-height:2.3;margin:.1rem 0}
.lm-wrap{text-align:center}
</style>
<div class="lm-wrap">
  <canvas id="ckCanvas" width="560" height="300" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:8px;max-width:100%;touch-action:none;cursor:grab"></canvas>
  <div class="lm-row">\(h_{ii}\)=<b id="ckH">–</b> (leverage if &gt; 2p/n=<b id="ckHt">–</b>) &nbsp;·&nbsp; \(|t_i|\)=<b id="ckT">–</b> (outlier if &gt; 2) &nbsp;·&nbsp; \(C_i\)=<b id="ckC">–</b> (infl. if &gt; 4/n=<b id="ckCt">–</b>)</div>
  <div class="lm-row">status: <b id="ckMsg">–</b> &nbsp; <button class="lm-btn" id="ckNew">↻ new data</button></div>
</div>
<script>
(function () {
  var cv = document.getElementById('ckCanvas'); if (!cv || cv.dataset.init) return; cv.dataset.init = 1;
  var c = cv.getContext('2d'), W = cv.width, H = cv.height, N = 16, p = 2, P = [], drag = -1;
  var XL = -3.4, XH = 3.4, YL = -3.4, YH = 3.4;
  function gauss() { var u = 0, v = 0; while (!u) u = Math.random(); while (!v) v = Math.random(); return Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * v); }
  function regen() { P = []; for (var i = 0; i < N - 1; i++) { var x = gauss() * 1.2; P.push([x, 0.8 * x + gauss() * 0.5]); } P.push([2.4, 0.3]); }
  function PX(x) { return 30 + (x - XL) / (XH - XL) * (W - 44); } function PY(y) { return H - 22 - (y - YL) / (YH - YL) * (H - 36); }
  function IX(px) { return XL + (px - 30) / (W - 44) * (XH - XL); } function IY(py) { return YL + (H - 22 - py) / (H - 36) * (YH - YL); }
  function pos(e) { var r = cv.getBoundingClientRect(); return [(e.clientX - r.left) * W / r.width, (e.clientY - r.top) * H / r.height]; }
  function ols(pts) { var n = pts.length, mx = 0, my = 0; pts.forEach(function (q) { mx += q[0]; my += q[1]; }); mx /= n; my /= n; var sxy = 0, sxx = 0; pts.forEach(function (q) { sxy += (q[0] - mx) * (q[1] - my); sxx += (q[0] - mx) * (q[0] - mx); }); var b1 = sxy / (sxx || 1e-9); return [my - b1 * mx, b1, mx, sxx]; }
  function draw() {
    var f = ols(P), b0 = f[0], b1 = f[1], xbar = f[2], Sxx = f[3], i, n = N;
    var ssr = 0; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { var e = P[i][1] - (b0 + b1 * P[i][0]); ssr += e * e; } var sig = Math.sqrt(ssr / (n - p));
    var sp = P[n - 1], hii = 1 / n + (sp[0] - xbar) * (sp[0] - xbar) / (Sxx || 1e-9), resi = sp[1] - (b0 + b1 * sp[0]);
    var ti = resi / (sig * Math.sqrt(Math.max(1e-6, 1 - hii))), Ci = (1 / p) * (hii / Math.max(1e-6, 1 - hii)) * ti * ti;
    var hThr = 2 * p / n, cThr = 4 / n, isLev = hii > hThr, isOut = Math.abs(ti) > 2, isInfl = Ci > cThr;
    var f2 = ols(P.slice(0, n - 1));
    c.clearRect(0, 0, W, H);
    c.strokeStyle = '#eee'; c.lineWidth = 1; c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(PX(XL), PY(0)); c.lineTo(PX(XH), PY(0)); c.moveTo(PX(0), PY(YL)); c.lineTo(PX(0), PY(YH)); c.stroke();
    c.strokeStyle = '#999'; c.setLineDash([5, 4]); c.lineWidth = 1.5; c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(PX(XL), PY(f2[0] + f2[1] * XL)); c.lineTo(PX(XH), PY(f2[0] + f2[1] * XH)); c.stroke(); c.setLineDash([]);
    c.strokeStyle = '#e74c3c'; c.lineWidth = 2.5; c.beginPath(); c.moveTo(PX(XL), PY(b0 + b1 * XL)); c.lineTo(PX(XH), PY(b0 + b1 * XH)); c.stroke();
    c.fillStyle = 'rgba(44,62,80,0.75)'; for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) { c.beginPath(); c.arc(PX(P[i][0]), PY(P[i][1]), 3.4, 0, 6.2832); c.fill(); }
    var col = isInfl ? '#e74c3c' : (isLev ? '#3b6fb6' : (isOut ? '#e67e22' : '#ffd23f'));
    c.fillStyle = col; c.strokeStyle = '#000'; c.lineWidth = 1.5; c.beginPath(); c.arc(PX(sp[0]), PY(sp[1]), 7, 0, 6.2832); c.fill(); c.stroke();
    document.getElementById('ckH').textContent = hii.toFixed(3); document.getElementById('ckHt').textContent = hThr.toFixed(3);
    document.getElementById('ckT').textContent = Math.abs(ti).toFixed(2); document.getElementById('ckC').textContent = Ci.toFixed(3); document.getElementById('ckCt').textContent = cThr.toFixed(3);
    var tags = []; if (isLev) tags.push('<span style="color:#3b6fb6">leverage</span>'); if (isOut) tags.push('<span style="color:#e67e22">outlier</span>'); if (isInfl) tags.push('<span style="color:#e74c3c"><b>influential</b></span>');
    document.getElementById('ckMsg').innerHTML = tags.length ? tags.join(' · ') : '<span style="color:#1b7a3a">typical point</span>';
  }
  cv.addEventListener('pointerdown', function (e) { var q = pos(e); if (Math.hypot(PX(P[N - 1][0]) - q[0], PY(P[N - 1][1]) - q[1]) < 16) { drag = N - 1; cv.style.cursor = 'grabbing'; } });
  window.addEventListener('pointermove', function (e) { if (drag < 0) return; var q = pos(e); P[drag] = [Math.max(XL, Math.min(XH, IX(q[0]))), Math.max(YL, Math.min(YH, IY(q[1])))]; draw(); e.preventDefault(); });
  window.addEventListener('pointerup', function () { drag = -1; cv.style.cursor = 'grab'; });
  document.getElementById('ckNew').addEventListener('click', function () { regen(); draw(); });
  regen(); draw();
})();
</script>
```

## Next

[Selection of Models](selection.qmd)
